content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLI'TICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Shelley Whitaker 
7251 Riverwind Way 
Sacramento CA 95831-3247 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-132 
Dear Ms. Whitaker: 
August 13, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government employment 
("revolving door") provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. After you retire from your job with the Department of General Services, but remain on 
the payroll to "run out" your leave credits, may you work as an independent contractor or as a 
subcontractor for a private firm? 
2. If so, are you permitted to work on a project involving a client agency of the Department 
of General Services if you never worked on the project while employed by the state? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. Yes. The Act does not prohibit you from taking a job with another employer after you . 
have ceased working for the state even though you remain on the state payroll to "run down" your 
leave credits. However, once you are no longer authorized to perform the duties of your job at the 
Department of General Services and you stop performing them, the Act's one-year ban and the 
permanent ban regarding post-governmental employment will apply to any work you do. 
2. Yes. However, the one-year ban will prohibit you from appearing as a paid consultant for 
the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-19-132 
Page No. 2 
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, 
or the sale or purchase of goods or property before the Department of General Services, or any 
other agency whose budget, personal, or operations are subject to the direction and control of the 
Department of General Services, for one year. To the extent you did previously participate in a 
specific project as a state employee, the permanent ban does not app1y to future participation in the 
project. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
You are currently employed as a Capital Outlay Program Manager with the State of 
California Department of General Services ("DGS") Real Estate Services Division. Before your 
current job, you served as a Supervising Project Director III and, before that, as a Project Director-
both at DGS. Your position is designated in DGS's conflict of interest code and you file a 
State~ent of Economic Interests (Form 700) annually. In your current role, you oversee staff who 
contract for design services and construction projects. You also supervise various design and 
construction projects for several state agencies. 
You plan on retiring from DGS at some point in 2019, but will continue to receive 
compensation for remaining leave credits after you stop working. After you have left the position, 
but while you are still receiving compensation for remaining leave credits, you would like to pursue 
consulting opportunities in your area of expertise. Specifically, you would like to work either as an 
independent contractor or as a sub-consultant to a larger firm providing design and construction 
services to The University of California, Hastings College of Law ("UC Hastings"), a client agency 
of DGS. UC Hastings has contracted for design and construction services to build student housing 
via a public private partnership (P3) and you expect that they will contract for additional services, 
including project management oversight-your specialty. 
While you have worked with UC Hastings in the past in your role at DGS, neither you nor 
DGS have participated in the solicitation, procurement, or contracting processes for UC Hastings' 
P3 project. Furthermore, unlike other state agencies, UC Hastings has statutory authority to contract 
for design and construction services without DGS. 
ANALYSIS 
Public officials are subject to two types of post-governmental employment restrictions under 
the Act: the one-year ban, and the permanent ban on "switching sides." Together, these provisions 
are commonly known as the "revolving door" prohibitions.2 
The One-Year Ban: 
The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from being paid to appear before or 
communicate with his or her former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or 
legislative actions or any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or 
2 Please note that, in addition to the one-year ban and the permanent ban, the Act also prohibits you from 
making or influencing governmental decisions prior to leaving state service that directly relating to a person with whom 
you are negotiating or have arrangements concerning future employment. (Section 87407; Regulation 18747). 


File No. A-19-132 
Page No. 3 
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. 
(Section 87406; Regulation 18746.1.) 
The one-year ban applies to state employees who hold positions that are designated in their 
agency's conflict-of-interest code, or that should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest 
code. (Section 87406(d)(l); Regulation 18746.l(a)(4).) The ban applies for twelve months from the 
date on which the employee permanently leaves state employment and, while in effect, applies only 
to former state employees who are paid to appear before or communicate with their former agency 
on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or representative of that person. (Section 87406(d)(l); 
Regulation 18746. l(b)(3) and (4).) 
An appearance or communication includes, but is not limited to, in person conversations and 
meetings, telephone conversations, as well as written and electronic correspondence. (Regulation 
18746.2.) An appearance or communication is considered for the "purpose of influencing" if it is 
made for the "principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, 
delaying, or advancing the action or proceeding." (Id). 
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited under the one-year ban if they are 
made: (1) before a state agency that the public official worked for or represented; (2) before a state 
agency whose "budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency 
the public official worked for or represented; or (3) before any state agency subject to the direction 
and control of the Governor if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office 
during the twelve months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation 
18746.l(b )(6)(C).) 
You are a designated employee of a DGS, a state agency. Therefore, the one-year ban will 
apply to you once you leave state service. For twelve months from the date on which you 
permanently leave state service, you will be prohibited from making paid appearances and 
communications before DGS for the purpose of influencing ~dministrative or legislative action, or 
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation 
of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. The one-year 
ban will also prohibit you from making appearances and communications before any state agency 
whose "budget, personnel, and other operations are subject to the control" of DGS. 
However, you will not be prohibited from making appearances or communications before 
DGS that are made as part of "services performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the 
· requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract, or sale agreement ... provided the 
services do not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or 
proceedings" for the twelve-month period commencing once you have permanently left state 
service. (Regulation 187 46.1 ( c) ). ) Furthermore, you will not be prohibited from performing work as 
an independent contractor or as a subcontractor that does not require you to appear before or 
communicate with DGS. We have also advised that a former agency official may, without violating 
the one-year ban, draft proposals on a client's behalf to be submitted to.the agency so long as the 
former employee is not identified in connection with the client's efforts to influence an 
administrative action. (Cook Advice Letter, No. A-95-321; Harrison Advice Letter, No. A-92-289.) 
Similarly, a former agency official may use his or her expertise to advise clients on the procedural 


File No. A-19-132 
Page No. 4 
requirements, plans, or policies of the official's former agency so long as the employee is not 
identified with the employer's efforts to influence the agency. (Perry Advice Letter, No. A-94-004.) 
The Permanent Ban: 
The "permanent ban" on "switching sides" prohibits a former state employee from being 
paid to work on proceedings that the employee participated in while working for the state. (Sections 
87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.) Like the one-year ban, the permanent ban applies once a 
state employee has permanently left state employment or taken a leave of absence. (Regulation 
18741.1 (a)(l ).) 
As its name suggests, the permanent han is a lifetime ban. It prohibits a former state official 
from making a paid appearance (formal or informal), or any other paid oral or written 
communications on behalf of another person, with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-
judicial, or other proceeding in which the employee participated while working for the state (such 
as a lawsuit, administrative hearing, or negotiation of a state contract). (Regulation 18741.l(a)(2).) 
Unlike the one-year ban, the permanent ban also prohibits aiding, advising, counseiing, consulting 
or assisting in representing another person before a state administrative agency in a proceeding in 
which the employee participated while employed by the state. (Id.) 
For purposes of the permanent ban, a former state official is considered to have 
"participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially 
through_ decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a 
substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential information .... " (Section 87400(d).) A 
former state official whq held a management position at a state administrative agency is deemed to 
have participated in a proceeding if: (1) the proceeding was pending before the agency during his or 
her tenure, and (2) the proceeding was under his or her supervisory authority. (Section 87400(d); 
Regulation 18741.l(a)(4).) However, proceedings are not considered to have been under a former 
state employee's "supervisory authority" merely because the state employee, as a supervisor, was 
responsible for general oversight of the administrative actions or functions of a program. If the 
responsibilities concerning the specific or final review of a proceeding are expressly delegated to 
other agency personnel, and the supervisor is not invo_lved in the actual proceedings, the supervisor 
is not said to have "supervisory authority." (Regulation 18741.l(a)(4)(A).) 
Thus, the permanent ban prohibits you from doing work on proceedings that you 
participated in while ~orking for DGS. This prohibition includes both appearing before or 
communicating directly with DGS on behalf of others, as well as aiding, advising, counseling, 
consulting or assisting in representing any other person before DGS in a proceeding involving 
specific parties that you previously worked on while employed at DGS. However, you have 
indicated that neither you nor DGS have played any part in the solicitation, procurement, 
contracting, or any other part of the UC Hastings P3 housing project. Therefore, based on the facts 
you have provided, the permanent ban would not prohibit you from providing consulting services to 
UC Hastings on the P3 housing project, whether as an independent contractor or as.a subcontractor 
for a private firm. 


When the Revolving Door Rules Take Effect: 
File No. A-19-132 
Page No. 5 
The permanent ban applies once a state employee "has permanently left state service or is on 
leave of absence." (Regulation 18741. l(a)(l).) For purposes of the permanent ban, the date on 
which an official permanently leaves office or employment or takes a leave of absence is defined as 
"the date on which the official is no longer authorized to perform the duties of the office or 
employment, and the official stops performing those duties, even if the official continues to receive 
compensation for accrued leave times." 
Similarly, the one-year han applies once a state employee "has left his or her state office or 
employment, which means he or she has either permanently left state service or is on leave of 
absence." (Regulation 18746. l(b)(l).) For purposes of the one-year ban, the date an official 
"permanently leaves stafe office or employment" is the date on which the official is no longer 
authorized to perform the duties of the position and stops performing the duties of the position. 
(Regulation 18746.4(b)(l).) A person shall not be deemed to have left office permanently because 
he or she is on a leave of absence or serves as an intermittent employee. However, a person shall be 
deemed to have left office permanently if the person merely receives compensation for accrued 
leave credits. (Regulation 187 46.4(b )(2).) 
Accordingly, you will have "permanently left state service" upon your last day of work at 
DGS, even if you receive compensation for remaining leave credits after you have stopped 
performing your duties. Therefore, both the one-year ban and the permanent ban will apply to you 
after your last day of work at DGS. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
Counsel 
By: 
Toren Lewis 
Counsel, Legal Division 
TAL:aja

you have provided, the permanent ban would not prohibit you from providing consulting services to
